coredata.dc:title: Single-phase static immersion-cooled battery thermal management system with finned heat pipes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Hai
Li, Wei
Xiong, Shusheng
Luo, Zihao
Ahmed, Muhammad
coredata.dc:description: Effective thermal management is of critical importance to the performance and safety of lithium-ion batteries. However, research on small and medium-sized battery packs remains scarce. This paper proposes a new immersion cooling method. It combines finned heat pipes with a single-phase static immersion fluid, achieving optimal battery pack homogeneity in existing studies while outperforming the performance of conventional immersion cooling. The method is particularly suitable for energy storage batteries and small and medium-sized battery pack cooling applications. This paper presents a verification of the cooling performance of the cooling method through a combination of simulation analysis and experimental validation. The results demonstrate that the cooling method can enable small and medium-sized battery packs to meet the heat dissipation requirements of 3C or even higher discharge rate under natural convection conditions. In the active cooling scenario, when the battery pack is discharged at a 3C multiplier, the cooling scheme of 9 finned heat pipes can reduce the maximum temperature of the flow-submerged cooled battery pack by 6.4 °C and the maximum temperature difference between cells by 5.3 °C. Even when the battery is discharged at an extreme multiplication rate of 6C, the maximum temperature of the battery pack can be maintained at approximately 60 °C, with a maximum temperature difference between cells of around 15 °C. Based on the aforementioned simulation and experimental results, this paper also validates and analyses the main factors affecting the cooling effect of the method, namely the type of submerged liquid and the number of finned heat pipes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Single-phase cooling
Static immersion cooling
Finned heat pipes
Active and passive cooling

Influence of the number and arrangement of heat pipes
Thermal performance under extreme operating conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison experimental graphs (a) natural convection cooling; (b) immersion liquid cooling without flow; (c) immersion liquid + heat pipe cooling without flow: (d) immersion liquid + finned heat pipe; (e) immersion liquid + finned heat pipe forced air cooling.

Thermocouple distribution and forced air cooling flow direction; (a&c) Natural air convection, natural convection of immersed liquid and natural convection of heat pipe thermocouple distribution side view and bottom view; (b&d) Natural convection of finned heat pipe and forced air cooling thermocouple distribution side view and bottom view, and wind direction.
Comparison of simulation and experimental results.
Schematic diagram of active cooling flow: (a) Flow-through immersion cooling; (b) Forced air-cooled finned heat pipes.
Comparison of the number of heat pipes and their arrangement.
Comparison of cooling effects of different immersion fluids.
Comparison of flow immersion cooling and forced air-cooled finned heat pipe under extreme operating conditions.
Multi-Channel Temperature Measurement under Different Cooling Methods: (a) Maximum temperature of different channels;(b) Maximum temperature difference between different channels.
Thermophysical parameters of different immersion liquid types [54].

head.ce:title: Single-phase static immersion-cooled battery thermal management system with finned heat pipes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Energy Engineering Zhejiang University, 310000 Hangzhou China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Energy Engineering Zhejiang University, 310000 Hangzhou China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Clean Energy and Carbon Neutrality of Zhejiang Province, 310014 Hangzhou China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Longquan Indttstrial Innovation Research Institute, 323050 Longquan District, Lishui Zhejiang Province, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Longquan Indttstrial Innovation Research Institute, 323050 Longquan District, Lishui Zhejiang Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hangzhou Wesail Technology Co, Ltd, 310000 Hangzhou, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hangzhou Wesail Technology Co, Ltd, 310000 Hangzhou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effective thermal management is of critical importance to the performance and safety of lithium-ion batteries. However, research on small and medium-sized battery packs remains scarce. This paper proposes a new immersion cooling method. It combines finned heat pipes with a single-phase static immersion fluid, achieving optimal battery pack homogeneity in existing studies while outperforming the performance of conventional immersion cooling. The method is particularly suitable for energy storage batteries and small and medium-sized battery pack cooling applications. This paper presents a verification of the cooling performance of the cooling method through a combination of simulation analysis and experimental validation. The results demonstrate that the cooling method can enable small and medium-sized battery packs to meet the heat dissipation requirements of 3C or even higher discharge rate under natural convection conditions. In the active cooling scenario, when the battery pack is discharged at a 3C multiplier, the cooling scheme of 9 finned heat pipes can reduce the maximum temperature of the flow-submerged cooled battery pack by 6.4 °C and the maximum temperature difference between cells by 5.3 °C. Even when the battery is discharged at an extreme multiplication rate of 6C, the maximum temperature of the battery pack can be maintained at approximately 60 °C, with a maximum temperature difference between cells of around 15 °C. Based on the aforementioned simulation and experimental results, this paper also validates and analyses the main factors affecting the cooling effect of the method, namely the type of submerged liquid and the number of finned heat pipes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

Specific volume heat production power of battery (
W /
m
3
)

kg /
m
3
)

Density of immersion liquid (
kg /
m
3
)

Specific heat capacity of immersion liquid (
kg /
m
3
)

ce:section.ce:para: With the energy crisis and environmental problems becoming increasingly significant, the development of new energy vehicles is receiving more and more attention [1]. Lithium-ion batteries have become the main power source for pure electric vehicles and energy storage batteries due to their high energy density, long cycle life, low self-discharge rate, and no memory effect [2]. However, heat dissipation issues still limit lithium-ion batteries' stability, reliability, and lifetime [3]. Research has shown that the optimal operating temperature of lithium-ion batteries should be between 20 and 40 °C, and the temperature difference between batteries should be less than 5 °C [4]. Batteries will age more quickly and have less capacity at cold temperatures. Elevated temperatures not only diminish battery performance but, in extreme circumstances, can result in thermal runaway, fire, and explosion. An excessive temperature differential can have an impact on the battery pack's homogeneity and hasten its aging process [5–7]. To solve the battery heat dissipation problem, researchers have proposed a variety of approaches, including air cooling, indirect liquid cooling, immersion cooling, phase change material (PCM) cooling, heat pipe (HP) cooling, and coupled cooling of the above approaches [8]. Based on whether the cooling process requires energy consumption or not, the above methods can be further categorized into active and passive cooling.

PCM cooling and HP cooling are both passive cooling methods, that do not need external energy supply during operation. Due to their low thermal conductivity and poor heat dissipation capacity, PCMs require cooling in conjunction with active heat dissipation methods [9]. Furthermore, the application of PCM cooling in commercial automobiles has been limited by concerns such as the risk of leakage [10]. In the last decade, heat pipes have been widely used in aerospace, electronics, and solar thermal management for their ability to operate under harsh conditions, high thermal conductivity, high efficiency, and compactness [11], and have also received great attention in the field of battery thermal management [12]. Research on heat pipe BTMS has been centered on enhancing the heat transfer contact area between the cell and the heat pipe [13–15]. This has led to the integration of heat pipes with phase change materials and different metal plates with various designs [16–19]. Compared with other cooling methods, air-cooling was widely used in pure electric vehicles (e.g., Nissan Leaf and Toyota Prius [20]) due to its simple structure, low cost, easy maintenance, and lightweight [21]. Still, liquid cooling has gradually replaced it due to its low cooling efficiency and poor temperature uniformity [22]. According to whether the coolant is directly in contact with the battery, liquid cooling can be categorized into indirect liquid cooling and immersion cooling. Indirect liquid cooling is cooled by the liquid flowing through the built-in channels such as cold plates or pipes, and the cold plates or pipes are in contact with the battery [23]. Studies have shown that indirect liquid cooling systems have greater cooling capacity and better cooling temperature homogenization capability than air cooling. Forced air cooling needs to consume 2–3 times more energy to maintain the same level of cooling as indirect liquid cooling [24]. In the cooling of large battery packs, such as those used in container energy storage, cold plate cooling is a widely used method. Indirect liquid cooling is also employed in new energy vehicles, including those produced by Tesla and Chevrolet [25]. In the research and application of indirect liquid cooling, researchers have developed numerous schemes and optimization paths through a series of measures, including the design of flow channels and the use of new cooling media [26]. However, indirect liquid cooling systems need to be coupled with vehicle air conditioning to form a second loop, and therefore, the cost, weight, and structural complexity of indirect liquid cooling systems are much greater than that of air cooling systems [27]. Immersion cooling, instead, directly immerses the battery into the dielectric fluid. The direct contact between the battery and the fluid makes immersion cooling more advantageous in the context of low contact thermal resistance, large heat transfer area, high cooling efficiency, and compact structure compared to indirect liquid cooling [28,29]. In addition, when the dielectric fluid is a flame retardant, immersion cooling can also effectively reduce the risk of spontaneous battery combustion [30]. However, the present flow immersion cooling also requires a second loop with the vehicle air conditioning, and due to the high viscosity of the dielectric fluid, the pump power required for the battery thermal management system (BTMS) is also higher [21].

As an emerging cooling method, immersion cooling has been widely used in Internet data centers and energy storage battery neighborhoods [10]. To prove the superior cooling performance of immersion cooling, researchers have made a series of efforts. Dubey et al [31] and Li et al [32] compared the cooling effect of immersion cooling and indirect liquid cooling on battery packs through numerical simulation and verified that immersion cooling has better heat dissipation properties. Bai et al [33] demonstrated the effectiveness of immersion cooling in preventing thermal runaway and thermal runaway propagation in batteries through experimental studies. Wu et al [34] used the immersion cooling method to design a new thermal management system for lithium-ion batteries with a high quality and volume integration ratio and experienced a better cooling effect than indirect liquid cooling. To further enhance the cooling effect of the immersion cooling method, researchers have proposed many solutions. By optimizing the dielectric fluids Li et al [35,36] designed an organic sealant wrapped around the surface of the battery and cooled the battery using hydro glycol solution. Gu [37], Tousi, and Najafi et al [38] achieved cooling optimization by using dielectric nanofluids. By increasing the phase transition, Zhou et al [39], Wang et al [40], and Li et al [41] set up a condenser on the battery module, where the immersion liquid is vaporized on the surface of the battery and condensed by the condenser to achieve more efficient and uniform heat dissipation. By optimizing the flow of submerged liquid there are droplet contact cooling system proposed by Zha et al [42], which optimizes the cooling effect of submerged cooling with the help of jet-enhanced heat dissipation. Han et al [43], achieved the optimization of immersion cooling performance by optimizing the fins between cells. However, compared with the above methods, the cooling methods proposed by Suresh Patil et al[28], which combine immersion cooling with air-cooling of the tab, and Wang et al [44], which couples immersion cooling with direct cooling, are more informative in practical battery thermal management applications due to their simple structure and relatively low realization cost and technical difficulty. Therefore the cooling method of immersion cooling coupled with other basic cooling methods is highly potential.

In summary, researchers have conducted a lot of studies on the cooling of batteries from different perspectives. Immersion cooling has the advantages of compact structure and high reliability, however, there are still some problems to be solved. Firstly, the field of passive heat dissipation for small and medium-sized battery packs is under-researched and in a state of relative neglect [48]. This not only restricts the use of high-rate charging and discharging applications such as supercharging of small and medium-sized battery packs but also poses a potential safety hazard to users of small and medium-sized battery packs. In recent years, electric bicycles powered by lithium-ion batteries have become a very popular mode of transportation for urban residents because of their convenience, economy, environmental protection, and other advantages. China's annual sales of e-bikes in 2023 exceeded 42 million units, with more than 350 million units currently in circulation. However, the number of fire accidents related to e-bikes has also shown a significant upward trend due to the lack of proper BTMS [45]. According to the Department of Fire and Rescue of China's Ministry of Emergency Management (MEM), there were 6,462 e-bike fire accidents nationwide in the first half of 2021 [46]. Electrical faults (overcharge and short circuit) and spontaneous combustion are the main causes of e-bike fires, accounting for 62.1 % and 23.5 % of the total number of e-bike fires, respectively. A significant proportion of new e-bike models released by major Chinese e-bike brands are now equipped with lithium-ion batteries, with the figure exceeding 20 percent [47]. Secondly, single-phase static immersion cooling and heat pipe cooling each possess distinctive advantages. This paper synthesizes these advantages to propose a novel optimization direction for battery-coupled cooling. The battery immersed in a flowing dielectric fluid shows excellent cooling performance. However, it requires a second loop with the automobile air conditioner, which adds chillers, pumps, valves, and additional piping, in addition to the higher pump power needed due to the greater viscosity of the dielectric fluid. In comparison to flow-through immersion cooling, non-flow-through immersion cooling is demonstrated to provide superior temperature uniformity and the most optimal cell uniformity of all cooling methods. Compared with the traditional flow immersion cooling method, the phase change cooling method without flow immersion liquid achieves better cell uniformity while strengthening the cell heat dissipation, but the pressure change of the phase change process puts higher requirements on the sealing and safety of the module. The high sealing requirement will be a considerable obstacle for BTMS and battery post-maintenance. In addition, the additional path of the condenser increases the complexity of the cooling system. The application of heat pipes in battery thermal management is a promising area of research, yet the problem of enhancing the heat transfer contact area between the battery and the heat pipe remains unresolved. This paper presents a novel single-phase static immersion-cooled BTMS based on finned heat pipes. The geometrical model is shown in Fig. 1 , while Fig. 2 illustrates the heat transfer process.

In comparison with the conventional natural convection cooling methodology for small and medium-sized battery packs, this approach continues to utilize the passive cooling technique of natural convection, yet it is capable of markedly enhancing the temperature uniformity and heat dissipation efficacy of the battery pack. Experimental data indicates a reduction in the maximum temperature of the battery (down by 15.69 °C), as well as a decrease in the temperature difference between batteries (down by 5.51 °C) after utilisation of the coupled cooling method presented herein. And compared to cold plate cooling, this paper compares the cooling results of cold plate cooling of 21,700 cylindrical cells in the article by [25]. Even when the batteries in this paper are discharged at a 3C multiplication rate and use the forced air cooling mode with 4 finned heat pipes, the maximum temperature rise of the battery pack is still almost 5 °C lower. The reason for this is that there are major limitations in the application of cold plate cooling for cylindrical batteries. When the cold plate is in contact with the top and bottom of the cylindrical battery, a thicker insulating layer is placed on the surface of the cold plate for safety reasons, which has a greater impact on the heat dissipation performance of cold plate cooling. When the cold plate is in contact with the side wall of the battery, it will face the same problem of strengthening the contact area as the heat pipe. Compared with the diamond-shaped battery, the cylindrical battery has the advantages of compact structure and low cost. It is widely used in the field of power batteries and energy storage batteries. This solution can therefore be used to cool large energy storage batteries, but is also particularly suitable for small and medium-sized battery packs with passive or active cooling.

The objective of this study is to validate the cooling effectiveness of the single-phase static immersion-cooled BTMS with finned heat pipes and to discuss the main influencing factors of the method. Section 2 presents the geometrical and mathematical models of the simulation process. Section 3 describes the experimental setup for the experimental process and compares the experimental results with the simulation data. Section 4 discusses the simulation and experimental results. Section 5 concludes the paper.

In this paper, EVE21700/50E batteries and 8 mm tubular heat pipes are used to design the geometrical model shown in Fig. 1, in which the scheme consists of upper and lower boxes, immersion liquid, 16 batteries, and 12 tubular heat pipes with fins at both the upper and lower ends. According to the working temperature of the battery, the system selects the low-temperature heat pipe with acetone as the working material. The boiling point of acetone under standard atmospheric pressure is 57℃, and the suitable working temperature of the low-temperature heat pipe using it as the working material is 20–120 °C, and the corresponding pressure is 0.25–6.70 bar. The fins at the immersed end of the heat pipe are directly in contact with the battery, and the contact surfaces are coated with thermal silica to minimize contact thermal resistance. The battery box provides the axial restraint and the finned heat pipe provides the radial restraint to jointly secure the battery. In the design of the finned heat pipe, it is important to consider that the surface of the submerged end fins must be coated with thermally conductive silicone grease. If the fin thickness is too thin, it may cause adhesion or bridging, which will affect the heat exchange between the fins and the submerged liquid. Furthermore, the submerged end fins must also play a role in fixing the battery, so the submerged end fins are thicker than the fins at the heat sink. The height of the heat pipe has been selected to be 120 mm, which is the standard length of heat pipe commonly purchased in the market. This design will result in the formation of a 40 mm high natural convection or forced air cooling area outside the battery pack. Given that the battery must be completely submerged in the submerged liquid, it is foreseeable that the energy density of the battery pack will be close to or lower than that of the traditional indirect liquid cooling. Nevertheless, the reduction of pumps and coolant flow circuits, in addition to the unique submerged liquid characteristics required, renders this solution capable of enhancing the cooling performance of the system while concomitantly reducing the cost of the system. Furthermore, the variable shape and number of heat pipes enable the solution to offer greater flexibility for the actual engineering applications. The results of experimental studies indicate that a fan with a power consumption of only 3 W can reduce the temperature of the battery pack by 7 °C and control the temperature rise of the battery pack to approximately 10 °C in an open environment. This represents a highly competitive solution in comparison to indirect liquid cooling and flow immersion cooling, which often require a load power of tens of watts.

The principle of heat dissipation is shown in Fig. 2, where the arrows indicate the direction of heat transfer. The heat generated by the battery is transferred to the submerged fins and the submerged liquid through heat conduction and heat convection respectively. The heat absorbed by the submerged liquid can be transferred to the submerged end of the heat pipe either through the submerged fins or directly. The heat absorbed by the fins from the submerged liquid and the cell is transferred directly to the heat pipe. Ultimately, the heat generated by the battery is discharged through the cooling end of the heat pipe to cool the battery. The package is flange-sealed on the top and bottom of the case to ensure that the immersion fluid does not leak. Both the upper and lower cases contain open-worked battery supports that provide radial restraint of the batteries as well as a preferred solution to the problems of the cooling area of the battery packs, internal flow of submerged fluid, and inter-cell connections. Considering the experimental equipment and safety issues, 16 batteries are connected in series. The interval between batteries is set to 2 mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery heat generation and heat pipe modeling
ce:section.ce:para:
The equation for the energy conservation of a cell in a cylindrical coordinate system can be written as:
(1)

ρ b
represents the density of the cell,
C p
represents the specific heat capacity of the cell,
λ
T , r
and
λ
T , z
represent the radial and axial thermal conductivities of the cell, respectively, and
q
represents the volumetric heat production rate of the cell. In the simulation process, the cell is assumed to be a lumped parameter model with a uniform internal material composition.

According to Bernardi et al [49], the heat generation equation of the battery can be expressed as:
(2)

where
I
represents the charging or discharging current, positive for charging and negative for discharging,
V
represents the volume of each cell,
U
OCV
is the open-circuit voltage of the cell,
U
is the operating voltage of the cell,
T
is the temperature of the cell, and
d
U
OCV
dT
is the entropy coefficient due to the electrochemical reaction in the cell.
R 0
and
R p
are the ohmic and polarised internal resistance of the battery, r denotes the radius of the battery and L is the height of the battery. The internal resistance of the battery is measured using impedance spectroscopy. The technical parameters of the battery are shown in Table 1 , [50]. Given that the objective of this study is to verify the cooling effect of the cooling method, it is not necessary to ensure the accuracy of the battery heat generation model. To streamline the calculations and minimize the experimental expenditure, the battery has been simplified as an equivalent homogeneous heat source, with the equivalent internal resistance maintained. This enables the heat generation power of the battery under different discharge multiples to be estimated using Eq. (3).
(3)
q =
I
2
R 0
The energy equation for a heat pipe is as follows:
(4)

HP
C
p , H P
∂ T
∂ t

HP
∇ T

The application of computational fluid dynamics (CFD) to the modeling of heat pipe operation necessitates the utilization of multiphase models to simulate boiling and condensation phenomena. One of the principal challenges in the development of such models is the determination of the distribution of the liquid and gas phases. The most prevalent numerical technique for this type of problem is the Volume of Fluid (VOF) model. However, difficulties often arise when modeling complex geometries, and thus it is possible to equate a heat pipe to a heat superconductor with relevant thermophysical conditions such as thermal conductivity [52]. Where
ρ
HP
,
C
p , H P
denote the density and specific heat capacity of the heat pipe, respectively;
k
HP
is the equivalent thermal conductivity of the heat pipe, which was set to 11000(
W / ( m ⋅ K )
), about 30 times that of copper [53]. Based on the weighted average of acetone and aluminum tubes,
ρ
HP
is 2123.8(
k g /
m
3
) and
C
p , H P
is 1015.8(
J / ( k g ⋅ K )
) [48].

In this paper, the commercial computational fluid dynamics software COMSOL Multiphysics 6.0 is used to simulate the heat transfer characteristics and influencing factors of this scheme. The governing equations in the fluid domain are as follows:

Continuity equation:
(5)
∂ ρ
∂ τ
+ ∇ ∙
( ρ v )
= 0

Momentum equation:
(6)
∂ ( ρ v )
∂ τ
+ ∇ ∙
ρ v
v = - ∇ p +
μ
∇
2
v
+ ρ g

Energy Equation:
(7)
ρ
c p
∂ t
∂ τ
+ ∇
( ρ
C p
v t )
= ∇ ∙
( λ ∇ t )

The study of Choi et al [54] showed that hydrofluoric ether showed the best cooling effect in flow immersion cooling, but in practical applications, for cost and maintenance considerations, transformer oil is mostly used for cooling, so transformer oil was chosen as the immersion fluid in this paper [44]. The physical parameters of transformer oil are shown in Table 2 .

During simulation calculations, the accuracy and speed of the calculations depend greatly on the number of meshes and solvers. In this paper, a free tetrahedral grid is used for the simulation process, and the grid independence is analysed by comparing the maximum average temperature during natural convection cooling, using COMSOL's built-in mesh tool and set up to draw the mesh with physical field control. As shown in Fig. 3 , in the case of 3C discharge, the number of grids changed from 68466 to 679446 the maximum temperature had a decreasing trend, but the trend slowed down significantly. A comparison of the overall temperature rise of nearly 30 °C reveals that the maximum temperature is observed to be 0.03–0.06 °C lower on the adjacent grid change gradient between 88335–679446. The temperature difference between 88335 and 679446 is 0.135 °C, which suggests that the number of grids between these two points is sufficient to verify the cooling performance of this scheme. Based on the consideration of accuracy and arithmetic power, 156644 level of meshing is chosen in this paper.

In addition to being applied to the cooling of large energy storage batteries, this solution is also particularly suitable for small or medium-sized cylindrical lithium-ion battery modules or batteries, such as those used to power drones, electric motorbikes and scooters. In the practical application of small or medium-sized cylindrical lithium-ion battery packs, cooling means are often not provided. The modules are sealed and cooled directly by natural convection, which is able to meet the heat dissipation requirements when the battery is operated at lower multipliers. However, with the development of technology, it is also inevitable that the discharge power of small and medium-sized lithium-ion battery packs will put forward higher requirements. This programme can provide a reference for this development. In order to verify the cooling characteristics of a single-phase static immersion-cooled BTMS based on finned heat pipes and to assess the effectiveness of the finned heat pipes and static immersion cooling method with coupling, five sets of experiments were conducted, namely, natural air convection, natural convection of immersion liquid, natural convection of heat pipe and immersion liquid, natural convection of finned heat pipe and immersion liquid, and forced convection of finned heat pipe and immersion liquid, have been designed, as shown in Fig. 5. The process of testing and data acquisition for the five sets of experiments is shown in Fig. 4 .

The experiments were conducted using a battery pack consisting of 16 EVE 21700/50E Li-ion batteries connected in series for 1C discharge from 100 % SOC to 20 %, which means that the battery pack was discharged at a constant current of 5A for 48 min. The surface temperature and module temperature distribution of the battery under different cooling methods are collected by multiplexed temperature measurement and thermal imager for comparison, to verify the effect of different cooling conditions on battery heat dissipation. Before the commencement of the experiment, it is imperative to ascertain the battery's capacity and internal resistance, to guarantee that it will be able to fulfill the requisite experimental demands. The parameters of the experimental equipment are shown in Table 3 .

In the experiment, a plastic bracket with a 23 mm cell spacing was used to fix the cells and connect them by nickel spot welding. For the consideration of experimental consistency and control variables, before the battery assembly, the thermocouples were attached to the surface of the battery at the positions as shown in Fig. 6
(a) & (c), and the plastic bracket at the upper end was punched according to the position of the heat pipe. The natural convection experiments were carried out by placing the battery pack directly into the acrylic rectangular box. After the natural convection experiment is completed, pour the transformer oil directly into the acrylic box for the immersion liquid natural convection experiment. After the immersion liquid natural convection experiment is completed, insert the heat pipe without fins in the through-hole position, and carry out the natural convection of the heat pipe and immersion liquid. After the experiment of natural convection of heat pipe and immersion liquid is completed, the battery pack needs to be disassembled, and the thermocouples are reattached according to the position of Fig. 6(b) & (d). The finned heat pipe was reassembled and fixed to the battery using thermal silica with a thermal conductivity of 1.2 W/(m∙K). After the assembly, the battery is also connected by welding. The linked battery packs were reused to perform two sets of experiments: natural convection of the finned heat pipe with the immersed liquid and forced convection of the finned heat pipe with the immersed liquid. During the experimental process of the above five sets of experiments, each set of experiments was repeated three times except for the forced air-cooling experiment, and the results were recorded. Forced air-cooled finned heat pipes need to be repeated six times, every two times into a group, each group following the wind direction as shown in Fig. 6(d) 1, 2 to change the direction of air-cooled flow and thermocouples relative position to obtain the temperature relationship between the cells. To minimize the potential for systematic errors, the wind direction is varied between 1 and 2. This allows for the measurement of temperatures at varying distances from the fan position.

It should be noted that the conditions for the fabrication of a new type of integrated heat pipe are not yet available. The team used thermal silica to connect 8 mm copper heat pipes with machined aluminum fins to make the finned heat pipes used in the experiment. The thermal performance of the self-made finned heat pipe was not very ideal due to the presence of contact thermal resistance and manual errors.

The results of the simulation, as depicted in Fig. 7 , were generated by the scheme depicted in Fig. 4(C). The battery was discharged at a magnification of 1C, while all other conditions remained consistent with those employed in the other simulation models. The maximum temperature of the battery pack can be observed in the comparison graph, and the discrepancy between the experimental and simulation results is within 3 %. Furthermore, the error of the average temperature of the battery pack is also comparable to the maximum temperature. The significant divergence between the batteries derived from the simulation and experimental results is also largely consistent with each other. Hence, it can be inferred that the simulation model is fundamentally reliable.

To verify the superiority of the finned heat pipe coupled with the immersion cooling scheme, this section compares the thermal performance of this cooling method with that of the conventional immersion cooling method in both passive and active cooling scenarios, respectively, through simulation verification. The performance of different cooling methods in terms of cooling efficiency and battery homogeneity is reflected by comparing three sets of data: the maximum temperature of the battery pack, the temperature difference of each single cell, and the maximum temperature difference between the cells. For volume and cost considerations, the method of cooling 16 cells with 9 heat pipes and 4 heat pipes is chosen in this section to compare with the conventional immersion cooling method. As shown in Fig. 8 , the geometrical model of cooling 16 cells with 9 heat pipes is illustrated.

In the passive cooling scenario, the cooling effect was compared in four cases simply submerging the cell in the submerged liquid without using heat pipes, using 9 heat pipes without fins on the submerged end of the heat pipes, using 9 heat pipes with fins on the submerged end of the heat pipes, and using 4 heat pipes with fins on the submerged end, respectively. The battery pack was discharged at a 3C current for 20 min, and the time step of the transient simulation was 40 s. The bottom surface of the model was set as an adiabatic boundary, and the rest of the surfaces were natural convection boundaries.

From Fig. 9 , it can be seen that in single-phase static immersion cooling after adding 9 finned heat pipes, the maximum temperature is reduced by 10 °C, the maximum temperature difference between cells is reduced by 3.85 °C, the average temperature difference of single-cell is reduced by 0.1 °C, but the maximum temperature difference of single-cell is increased by 0.234 °C. In addition, the presence or absence of fins at the submerged end has a significant effect on the cooling effect of the finned heat pipe. The maximum temperature of the cell is reduced by about 3 °C with the addition of fins at the submerged end of the heat pipe, the maximum temperature difference between cells is reduced by 0.523 °C, and the maximum temperature difference of single-cell is almost the same, the average temperature difference of single-cell is reduced by 0.19 °C. When the battery pack is cooled by the four finned heat pipes, it also exhibits a cooling performance that is superior to that of simple immersion cooling. The maximum temperature is reduced by 5.67 °C, the maximum temperature difference between cells is reduced by 3.46 °C, but the maximum temperature difference of single-cell is increased by 0.393 °C, the average temperature difference of single-cell is increased by 0.1 °C. In the case of passive cooling, although the cooling performance of both 9- and 4-finned heat pipes can be significantly better than simple immersion cooling, the cooling performance of 9-finned heat pipes is significantly better than that of 4. Considering the cost, volume, and application scenarios, using the natural convection cooling method with 9 finned heat pipes is more cost-effective. Overall, when the battery pack is discharged at a rate of 3C and is in a passive cooling environment, the immersion cooling method with the addition of 9 finned heat pipes, compared with the single-phase static immersion cooling, although it will increase the maximum temperature difference of single-cell by 0.234 °C. Still, it can reduce the average temperature of the battery pack by 10 °C and reduce the maximum temperature difference between cells by 3.85 °C. Therefore, the cooling solution with 9 finned heat pipes is more cost-effective in passive cooling scenarios.

When the battery pack was under active cooling conditions, the cooling effects of flow immersion cooling and forced air-cooled finned heat pipe were compared. The flow distribution of immersion fluid and air is shown in Fig. 10 , where the arrows indicate the flow direction of the fluid. The flow rate of flow immersion cooling is 0.2 m/s, the diameter of the inlet and outlet is 10 mm, and the flow rate of air is 4 m/s.

As evident from Fig. 11 , active cooling with fins on the immersion end demonstrates superior heat dissipation performance compared to the absence of fins in all three aspects: average temperature, single-cell temperature difference, and inter-cell temperature difference, among them, the maximum temperature and single-cell temperature difference show the most significant improvement, with a reduction of 16.61℃ and 4.55℃, respectively. In comparison with flow immersion cooling, both forced air cooling with 4 and 9 finned heat pipes showed better cooling effects. Compared to flow immersion cooling, the maximum temperature of the 9 finned heat pipes decreased by 6.4 °C, the temperature difference between cells decreased by 5.3 °C, and the maximum temperature difference of single-cell decreased by 0.55 °C, but the average temperature difference of single-cell increased by 0.38 °C. The maximum temperature of the 4 finned heat pipes decreased by 3.964 °C, the temperature difference between cells decreased by 5.44 °C, the maximum temperature difference of single-cell decreased by 0.17 °C, but the average temperature difference of single-cell increased by 0.96 °C. In the case of active cooling, the cooling performance of 9 and 4 finned heat pipes is significantly better than that of flow immersion cooling, and the cooling performance of 9 finned heat pipes is significantly better than that of 4 finned heat pipes. However, considering the cost, volume, cooling effect, and application scenarios, the forced air cooling method with 4 finned heat pipes can already meet the cooling requirements of most batteries, so the cooling of 4 finned heat pipes is more cost-effective. However, for some battery packs with special requirements, it is still necessary to use a greater number of heat pipes for cooling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the number and arrangement of heat pipes
ce:section.ce:para: In the single-phase static immersion cooling system based on heat pipes, the heat pipes bear the main task of heat transportation to the outside. Obviously, the number and arrangement of heat pipes affect the heat dissipation effect of the coupling method. In this section, the effects of the number of heat pipes and their arrangement on the cooling effect are verified by comparing the three arrangement schemes of 4, 9, 12, and 17 heat pipes under the conditions of forced air-cooling and battery pack at 3C discharging.

The comparison of 4 and 9 heat pipes has been stated in detail in the previous section and will not be pursued here. As can be seen from the Fig. 12 , the 12 heat pipes show better cooling performance compared to the 9 heat pipes in maximum temperature, single-cell temperature difference, and inter-cell temperature difference. The maximum temperature is reduced by 3.351 °C, the average single-cell temperature difference is reduced by 0.833 °C, and the temperature difference between cells is reduced by 1.593 °C. However, there is not much difference when comparing 17 to 12. The maximum temperature is reduced by 0.122 °C, the average single-cell temperature difference is reduced by 0.186 °C, and the temperature difference between cells is reduced by 0.01 °C. Therefore, it can be concluded that increasing the number of heat pipes can strengthen the cooling performance of the coupling method, but the performance improvement is limited.

In immersion cooling, a variety of dielectric fluids have been used in the study of BTMS systems, and representative dielectric fluids and main properties have been summarized in Table 4 . In this section, the effects of dielectric fluid type and thermophoretic properties on the thermal performance of the cooling method in this paper will be compared. A forced air-cooled 9-fin heat pipe model with 4 m/s is used in the simulation process, as shown in Fig. 10(b).

Fig. 13 shows the comparison curves of the average temperature, single maximum temperature difference, and inter-cell maximum temperature difference at the end of the 3C discharge of the battery pack under different immersion fluids. Comparing the physical parameters of different immersion fluids shown in Table 4, we can conclude the following. From the physical property table we can see that mineral oil and ester have the same density and thermal conductivity, as well as similar specific heat, but the viscosity of mineral oil is three times that of ester, and from the cooling effect, the cooling effect of both is almost the same, so in this paper's cooling method, the viscosity of the submerged liquid has a weak effect on the heat dissipation; By comparing the physical properties and cooling effect of silicone oil and hydrofluoroethers, in the case of fluoride with higher specific volume heat capacity and lower thermal conductivity, silicone oil can realize better cooling effect no matter in the average temperature or in the temperature difference of the battery, it can be concluded that the thermal conductivity of the submerged liquid has a more significant effect on the cooling performance of the submerged liquid; A comparison of the heat dissipation effects of olefin and silicone oil reveals that the specific volume heat capacity of the submerged liquid also has a significant impact on the cooling of the battery pack. The above comparison results demonstrate that heat conduction is the primary mechanism for absorbing and transferring the heat generated by the battery in the submerged liquid. Furthermore, the spontaneous flow of the submerged liquid due to the temperature difference is minimal. Consequently, the utilization of novel submerged fluids with high thermal conductivity, such as nano-dielectric fluids, or the incorporation of mechanical perturbations in the submerged fluid, may be considered to further enhance the heat dissipation performance of the battery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal performance under extreme operating conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The advancement of technology and the growing intricacy of the operational scenarios of power/energy storage batteries will present a novel challenge to the heat dissipation performance of the BTMS system. The battery heat generation power of the 6C multiplier is derived from Eq. (3). The following data plots were obtained by comparing the same flow-immersion cooling and forced-air-cooled finned heat-pipe cooling schemes as those in Section 4.1.3. The battery is discharged at a 6C multiple rate, the total time is 600 s, and the time step is 20 s.

As can be seen in Fig. 14, the flow immersion cooling and forced air-cooled finned heat pipe exhibit similar comparative results to section 4.1.3 under extreme operating conditions. The maximum temperature of the forced air-cooled finned heat pipe was reduced by 23.242 °C, and the temperature difference between cells was reduced by 16.06 °C. The average temperature difference of the cells was increased by 3.44 °C, but the maximum temperature difference of single-cell was reduced by 4.632 °C, and there was a significant improvement in the cell homogeneity. In addition, under the extreme discharging condition of 6C, the maximum temperature of the flow immersion battery has already exceeded 80 °C, while the forced air-cooled finned heat pipe can still control the average temperature of the battery at about 60 °C, which demonstrates its superior heat dissipation capability. The simulation model presented in this paper reveals that the trend of the maximum temperature difference between the cells can be divided into three stages. The first stage, which is characterized by a slow growth, is followed by a rapid growth stage and finally a stabilization stage. The rapid growth stage, in turn, can be further subdivided into three phases: the early accelerated growth, the middle of the uniform growth, and the end of the decelerated growth. From the temperature difference between the batteries as illustrated in Fig. 14 , it can be observed that the temperature difference between the batteries with flow immersion cooling is still in the middle of the rapid growth stage, while the temperature difference between the batteries With non-flow immersion cooling, the temperature difference has already reached the end of the rapid growth stage and is approaching the stage of stabilization. This further demonstrates the significant role of the cooling method proposed in this paper in reducing the temperature difference of the battery pack.

The data obtained from the multi-channel temperature measurement instrument in the experiment are shown in Fig. 15 , with a sampling frequency of 1 Hz. Compared to natural convection cooling, the addition of a single-phase static immersion coolant reduces the maximum temperature of the battery by 11.88 °C, and the temperature difference between the batteries is reduced by 4.97 °C. The addition of a heat pipe to the single-phase static immersion coolant further reduces the average temperature of the battery by 2.96 °C, and the temperature difference is reduced by 0.24 °C. After adding heat pipes to single-phase static immersion cooling in simulation, the maximum temperature difference between cells increases by 2.86 °C, which is inconsistent with the experimental temperature difference between cells decreases by 0.25 °C after the addition of the heat pipe. After replacing the heat pipe with a homemade finned heat pipe in the experimental test, the maximum temperature is only reduced by 0.95 °C, and the temperature difference between the cells is only reduced by 0.3 °C. In comparison to the enhancement of the immersion cooling effect by the addition of the heat pipe, the enhancement effect of the fins is not immediately apparent. However, given the suboptimal contact between the fins and the cells and between the fins and the heat pipe, it can be postulated that the fins and the heat pipe possess a higher thermal conductivity than the aluminum heat pipe utilized in the simulation. The experimental results are found to be in alignment with the simulation results.

To further verify the cooling effect of the single-phase static immersion-cooled BTMS based on finned heat pipes proposed in this paper, the average temperature of the battery pack was successfully controlled to be below 30 °C, the temperature rise was controlled to be less than 10 °C, and the temperature difference between the cells was controlled to be around 2 °C at 1C discharge multiplication rate after the homemade experimental model was subjected to forced air-cooling and dissipation of heat by using a fan with a power of 3 W in an open environment. The maximum temperature was reduced by 10.12 °C compared to the single-phase static immersion cooling scheme, and the average temperature difference increased by only 0.64 °C. In the case of the unsatisfactory cooling effect of the finned heat pipe, this scheme can still realize the efficient cooling of the battery pack, so it can be concluded that this cooling method can effectively cool the battery.

The thermal imaging results collected during the experiment at the termination of discharge are shown in Fig. 16 . From the thermal imaging of air natural convection and immersion liquid natural convection, it can be seen that the temperature difference between the core and the edge of the battery pack is significantly reduced after the addition of the immersion liquid and the temperature homogenization performance is better. Comparing the top-view and side-view thermograms of the HP natural convection and the finned HP natural convection, it can be seen that the temperature distribution of the submerged liquid in the heat pipe with fins is more uniform, so it can be shown that the fins can play a role in optimizing the heat transfer between the heat pipe and the submerged liquid in the experiment. By observing the top view and side view thermography of the finned heat pipe forced convection cooling, it can be seen that the temperature at the core of the side view is lower as compared to the edge, indicating that the finned heat pipe plays a good cooling role in the cooling of the submerged liquid. Thus the thermographic images can also illustrate that the cooling method has a good cooling effect.

This paper presents a new immersion cooling method. It combines finned heat pipes with a single-phase static immersion fluid to achieve the best battery pack uniformity in existing studies, with cooling performance superior to that of the conventional immersion cooling method. This method can simultaneously satisfy the application of both natural convection and forced air cooling scenarios. It addresses the lack of effective passive cooling methods for small and medium-sized battery packs in existing studies. In this paper, the cooling method is verified through a combination of simulation and experiment. It is concluded that the method can allow small and medium-sized battery packs to meet the heat dissipation requirements at 3C and even higher multiplicity under natural convection conditions. In the active cooling scenario, when the battery pack is discharged at a 3C multiplication rate, the cooling scheme comprising 9 finned heat pipes can reduce the maximum temperature of the conventional flow immersion-cooled battery pack by 6.4 °C and the maximum temperature difference between batteries by 5.3 °C. Even when the batteries are discharged at an extreme multiplication rate of 6C, the maximum temperature of the battery pack can be maintained at approximately 60 °C, while the maximum temperature difference between the batteries can be controlled to a value of around 15 °C. After a comprehensive analysis of the costs and benefits, it has been determined that a cooling scheme comprising nine finned heat pipes is optimal for natural convection conditions, while a cooling method with four finned heat pipes is most suitable for forced air cooling. In addition to investigating the impact of the number of heat pipes on the cooling efficacy of the method, this paper also assesses the influence of the type of submerged liquid. Due to the different cooling methods, the method requires less flow characteristics of the submerged liquid but has a stronger dependence on the thermal properties such as specific heat capacity and thermal conductivity, etc. In terms of the existing types of submerged liquids, this change can greatly reduce the cost of the practical application of this method, and is therefore of great value for application.
This research was funded by Zhejiang Province Spearhead and Leading Goose Research and Development Key Program [2023C01239]

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal runaway characteristics of LFP batteries by immersion cooling
